speaker: interviewer
text: Was rolling in front.

speaker: interviewer
text: You described to me the two miracles that happened to you, which enabled you to be saved. Is there anything that you have to think about, any philosophy that you have to think about as a result of what happened to you? Or anything that you have thought that it was important to tell your children or to tell the world about your experiences and what it meant for the rest of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think it was fate. I was-- I was predestined to survive it. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Any message? No, no. I blocked everything out for many, many years to be able to go on living. And for today, I just dragged it out. And [LAUGHS] as you can hear on my voice and on my behavior, I am very, very nervous about it. And I didn't sleep for the last few nights just to think about it and just to talk about it because I never do that. You can't live when you think back. You can't-- you couldn't live when you-- if I wouldn't have blocked it out, I couldn't have gone on living. The experiences, the humiliations, the degradations, the-- everything what happened, you just have to push it back, push it back. Not forget it, but to push it back. OK, we're going to stop the filming now and then in a minute or two, we'll call in your family and we'll put them on film as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Penelope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Golan. Thank you, Mrs. Golan, for all your help this afternoon. I see here now you've brought your family in. Would you like to introduce them and tell us a little bit about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my new family. I lost all the old one. And here we are, a new family. That is my daughter Vera, who has got a husband, Peter Reiner, but he can't be here. He's at work. And we have got two grandchildren. The older girl is Karen, and the young boy is Robby-- Robert. And to tell something about it, you don't have such a long tape, which will take all the things which I can tell [LAUGHTER] about them. I'm very proud of them, and very happy with them. They are wonderful kids and wonderful family to me. Especially since I lost my husband 15 months ago, they are everything to me. And they are beautiful to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Golan tell me, your grandchildren-- still at school or at university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karen is at university, and Robika is in year 11. He will do his leaving next year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are you studying, Karen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm doing arts in South Wales University.

speaker: interviewer
text: And specializing in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spanish and in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me, have you heard your grandmother's story, or will this be the first time that she's told it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps it'll be the first time that I've heard it, the whole story. She's-- many times over the years she's told me sort of little snippets of things that she's been through and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she asked me-- she told me when I visited Israel and I visited the Holocaust Museum and that affected me a lot. And she asked me to say something for her family when I visited there. And I've been reading a-- an autobiography of a lady who went through something very similar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva Quitnner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in Auschwitz as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in the next barrack to me. She wrote the book, And I just said to the children that if we would change her name to my name and her-- her city to Levice or Leva, I could have written the book myself. So Karen was just finishing that book and she-- she cried a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Robby, tell me, have you heard the story or have you just heard little bits?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't heard anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this will be completely new for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this will be very new. And I'm glad that she's done it. And I'm proud of her for doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, Karen, were you in favor of your mother doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vera.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's Vera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vera, I beg your pardon, Vera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. I'm really very pleased and very proud of her. I think it's something very difficult to do, not just as a account of her own life, a difficult part of her life, but I think it's something that she's leaving for future generations, not just to her descendents but to everyone, Jewish and non-Jewish. Stories like this should be heard and must be heard. And I think she's done something very courageous and very important for herself and for us as well. And I think she's wonderful. Love you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
